Disagree.

The policy covers damage caused when first responders force entry **because of an emergency or perceived emergency involving the policyholder**. Here, Joanne knew the crashing was just her dogs playing and called police to help subdue them, so there was no emergency (or perceived emergency) involving Joanne that prompted the forced entry under the policy definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.** The police (first responders) forced entry into Joanne’s home because of an emergency or perceived emergency involving Joanne (a suspected burglary), and the forced entry caused the damage—so it fits the policy’s definition of “Emergency Damages.”

**Agree.** The forced entry was by first responders because of a **perceived emergency involving Joanne** (her report of a suspected burglar), so the damage fits the policy’s definition even though no actual burglar was present. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage only when **“first responders”** force entry due to an emergency involving Salma. Sam is **an accountant and a friend**, not a first responder (e.g., police, fire, EMS). So the forced-entry damage doesn’t fit the definition of “Emergency Damages,” even though there was a real burglar.

**Agree.** The police (first responders) forced entry because of an emergency involving Salma (a burglar in her home), and the resulting damage falls within the policy’s definition of “Emergency Damages.”

Disagree.

The policy covers damage caused when **first responders** force entry **because of an emergency involving the policyholder**. Here, the forced entry was made by **Sam, an off-duty firefighter acting as a friend**, not responding in an official first-responder capacity. So the damage doesn’t fall within the policy’s definition of “Emergency Damages.”
Disagree.

Agree

The policy covers “oil leaking from a fixed oil-fired heating installation,” and Madison observed oil dripping from her heater (a fixed heating installation) and causing damage, so this fits the definition of “Escape of Oil.”

Disagree.

The policy covers “oil leaking from a fixed oil-fired heating installation.” Here, the oil “rush[ed] out” because of a mistake during a repair attempt (an accidental discharge), which doesn’t fit the ordinary meaning of “leaking.”

**Disagree.** The policy covers oil leaking from a **fixed oil-fired heating installation**. A **portable oil lamp** isn’t a fixed heating installation, so the damage wouldn’t fall under the “Escape of Oil” definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Agree.

The damage resulted from oil leaking from a **fixed oil-fired heating installation** (the cracked heater), which matches the policy’s definition of “Escape of Oil.”

**Disagree.** The policy covers oil leaking from a **fixed oil‑fired heating installation**. A wall‑mounted oil lamp is not an oil‑fired heating installation, so the damage wouldn’t fall under “Escape of Oil” as defined. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Disagree.

Agree

Final answer is: **Agree**

Agree.

The damage was caused by a sudden, unforeseen flow of water from an internal source (a leaking pipe), and it occurred over several hours—still consistent with an “escape of water” event rather than long-term gradual seepage. Final answer is: **Agree**
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**
Agree. Final answer is: **Agree**
**Agree.** The leak was a sudden, unforeseen flow of water from an internal source (the water cooler, akin to a faulty appliance), and it caused damage to the building. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers a sudden, unforeseen flow of **water** from an internal source (e.g., burst pipe or faulty appliance). Here, the substance that escaped and caused the damage was **paint**, not water, so it doesn’t fit the definition of “Escape of Water” as stated. Final answer is: **Disagree**
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**

**Disagree.** The policy covers a sudden flow of **water** from an internal source (e.g., burst pipe/faulty appliance). Here, the leaking substance is **tomato juice**, not water, so it likely falls outside the defined “Escape of Water” coverage. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers only “damage … caused by **naturally-occurring** fire.” Here, the fire resulted from a man-made event (a defective propane tank explosion from a gas grill), not a naturally occurring fire.

Disagree.

The policy covers only “damage … caused by naturally-occurring fire.” Here, the garage fire was caused by a brush fire spreading onto the property (a naturally occurring fire), but then the propane tank explosion occurred after the fire reached the grill. The damage to the garage occurred before the grill explosion, so it was caused by the brush fire and would fit. However, because the definition is limited and ambiguous about whether a brush fire counts as “naturally-occurring,” and many brush fires are not naturally caused, the safer reading is that coverage is not established on these facts alone. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.**

The garage damage was caused by a **naturally-occurring fire** (the brush fire): embers from that fire hit the grill, triggering an explosion that spread fire to the garage. Even though the grill explosion was part of the chain of events, the initiating cause fits the policy’s definition of “Fire.”

Disagree.

The policy covers flooding only when it’s caused by a “sudden release from outside the buildings.” Here, the water came from a leaking waterbed inside the home, so it doesn’t meet the definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Agree.

The damage fits the policy’s definition of “Flooding” because there was an invasion of the property by a large volume of water caused by a sudden release from outside the building (the hydrant being struck).

Disagree.

The policy defines “Flooding” as water caused by a sudden release **from outside the buildings**. Here, the water came from Charlotte’s own rain barrel next to the home (a stored source), not an external flood-type event, so it likely wouldn’t meet the policy’s definition of “Flooding.”

Disagree.

The policy defines “Flooding” as a **rapid build-up** of a large volume of water from outside the building. Here, the creek level rose **gradually over several years**, so the damage doesn’t fit the “rapid build-up” requirement. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Agree. Final answer is: **Agree**
Disagree.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers **damage to plants** caused by **civil commotion, strikes, or labor and political disturbances** affecting the plants themselves. Here, the strike caused a **store closure and inconvenience**, and the plants died due to **lack of fertilizer** (an indirect, consequential loss), not because the plants were physically damaged by the strike or disturbance.

**Agree.** The plants are within her property boundaries and the damage was caused by strike-related activities (picketing spilling onto her land), which fits the policy definition (“caused by civil commotion, strikes, or labor and political disturbances”).

Disagree.

The policy covers **damage caused by** civil commotion, strikes, or labor/political disturbances. Here, the strike caused a **fertilizer supply unavailability**, and the plants died due to lack of fertilizer—not from direct strike-related physical damage to the plants themselves. This is an indirect/consequential loss, which does not fit the stated definition of covered “damage.”

Disagree.

The policy covers damage caused by “malicious people or vandals.” A neighbor’s dog trampling plants is animal-caused damage, not damage caused by malicious people or vandals under the given definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Agree

The plants were within her property boundaries and the damage was caused by masked individuals intentionally trampling them, which fits “malicious people or vandals” under the Garden Plants damage definition.

Disagree.

The policy covers damage caused by “malicious people or vandals.” Middle school children trampling plants could be accidental or careless, but the definition requires malicious/vandalism intent, which isn’t established from the facts given. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers missed employment due to injuries that occur **under regular working conditions**. Gavin’s injury happened while off work doing a personal activity (putting up holiday lights and free climbing his house), not under his regular working conditions as an A/C repair technician.

**Agree.** Gavin’s injury occurred while performing his job under normal working conditions, and it caused him to miss employment for weeks, which fits the policy’s definition of covered “General Damages” for missed work due to injury. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers missed employment due to injuries that occur **under regular working conditions**. Gavin’s injury happened while helping his girlfriend at her home, not while performing his job for work under his regular employment conditions. Therefore, his lost income would not fall within that defined coverage.
Disagree.

Disagree.

The policy covers accidental damage from “Hot Work” only when the work is on Shannon’s **home or belongings**. Welding on her **motorcycle** (not described as part of the home or covered belongings under this definition) doesn’t clearly fit that requirement, even though it involves a flame and ignited flammable materials.

**Agree.** The work Shannon was doing (repairing her motorcycle using a gasoline-powered air compressor) **used and produced heat**, and that heat **ignited flammable materials**, fitting the policy’s definition of “Hot Work.” Final answer is: **Agree**

Disagree.

Using a heat gun to dry glue qualifies as “Hot Work,” but the damage to the dining table is heat damage (warping), not ignition-related accidental damage from “Hot Work” as defined (“heat that could ignite flammable materials”). Based on that definition, this loss wouldn’t be covered under the Hot Work coverage. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

Using a heat gun to dry flooring glue is “Hot Work” because it uses a heat source that could ignite flammable materials. The fire damage arose from that hot work activity, so it would fall under the policy’s “Hot Work” accidental damage coverage, meaning she **is** covered.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Disagree.

Disagree.

The definition covers “damage to belongings caused while being removed … from the home.” Denise’s items were damaged during a truck accident while in transit, not during the removal from the home itself, so this loss doesn’t fit the stated “House Removal” coverage. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage **only when the belongings are being removed by “professional removal contractors.”** Denise’s uncle, although formerly a professional mover, is helping her as a private individual—not acting as a contracted professional removal company—so the move doesn’t fit the definition of “House Removal” under the policy.
Disagree. Harper’s belongings were stored by her cousin Bill, not by **professional removal contractors**, so the situation doesn’t fit the policy’s definition of “House Removal” coverage.
**Agree.** The damage occurred while Harper’s belongings were being **stored by professional removal contractors** (the moving company), which falls within the policy’s definition of “House Removal.”

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage to belongings **while being stored by professional removal contractors**. Here, Harper asked Bill to store the items **in his personal office**, not as storage provided in his capacity as a **professional removal contractor** (and not in professional storage arranged through the moving company). So the loss doesn’t clearly fall within the defined “House Removal” coverage.

Disagree.

Although Jason’s losses stem from identity theft, the theft was “connected with the policyholder’s business” because his Social Security number was stolen through the car dealership’s computer network (his workplace/business). That triggers the policy exclusion, so the claim would not be covered under this definition.

Disagree.

Even though the theft happened at home, Jason’s social security number was used to open fraudulent lines of credit—activity connected to his business role as a successful car salesman (credit/financing is commonly part of car sales). Because the policy excludes “identity theft connected with the policyholder’s business,” the claim would fall within the exclusion. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

Although the theft occurred while Jason was at home, the hackers accessed his **work laptop** and stole his information in a way that is **connected with his business/employment**, which falls under the policy’s exclusion for “identity theft connected with the policyholder’s business.”

**Disagree.**

The identity theft is **connected with Jack’s occupation** because it arose from hackers infiltrating **his office/business network**, which the policy **expressly excludes**. Therefore, Jack would not be covered under this Identity Theft policy.

Disagree.

The loss arose from hackers stealing Jack’s credit card information via his home WiFi, which is ordinary personal identity theft and not “connected with [his] occupation” as a business consultant. Because the only stated limitation is the occupational-connection exclusion, and the facts don’t tie the theft to his work, the exclusion shouldn’t apply—so the statement “Jack is covered” is true.

Disagree.

Although Jack suffered identity theft, the loss is connected with his occupation because his credit card information was stolen through a restaurant network that he and his colleagues regularly use for business meetings, triggering the policy’s exclusion for “identity theft connected with the policyholder’s occupation.”

**Disagree.**

The policy applies only if the damage occurs **“while temporarily away from home.”** Vivian moved abroad **permanently**, so she was not temporarily away when the fire happened. Therefore, the Loss and Accidental Damage coverage would not apply. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Disagree.

Disagree.

The policy applies only if the damage occurs **while the property is “temporarily away from home.”** Here, the damage occurred **to Vivian’s home itself while she was living abroad**, not to property taken away from the home. Keeping the deed and intending to return doesn’t make the home “temporarily away from home,” so this loss wouldn’t fall under that coverage definition.

Disagree.

The policy includes key replacement **only in the case of theft**. Peter **lost** the key (no theft is stated), so this situation doesn’t meet the policy’s definition for coverage.

Disagree.

The policy covers **key replacement in the case of theft** under “Loss or Damage to a Goods Carrying Vehicle.” Here, the key was taken in a **mugging (robbery)**, not described as **theft** in the policy definition provided, so it doesn’t clearly fall within the stated coverage.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers **key replacement only “in the case of theft.”** Here, the key was **not stolen** in the ordinary sense described; it was **not returned due to forgetfulness/non-responsiveness**, which is closer to loss or misplacement (or a civil dispute) than theft. Therefore, Peter’s key replacement cost would not be covered under that specific provision.
Disagree.
Disagree.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**
Disagree.
Agree.

Disagree.

The damage was deliberate, but it was caused by Beth’s estranged son rather than an “adverse third party” as required by the policy definition, so it does not fit the covered category of “Malicious Acts or Vandalism” under that wording.

Disagree.

Her injury happened while loading groceries into the car, not “while traveling in your car,” so it likely falls outside the policy’s definition of a covered “Personal Accident.”
Agree.

Disagree.

The policy defines “Personal Accident” as “accidental injury while traveling in your car.” Gwen’s injury occurred after she had parked and was opening the car door, not while she was traveling.

Disagree.

Although Penelope’s injury occurred while traveling in her car, the policy requires the injury to result in a **permanent and total loss of use** of a limb. Her knee injury was temporary and fully resolved after three months of therapy, so it does not meet the policy’s definition of “Personal Accident” for this lost-income coverage.

**Agree.**

Penelope’s injury happened **while traveling in her car** and resulted in a **permanent and total loss of use** of her left knee. A knee is part of a **limb (the leg)**, so this fits the policy’s definition of “Personal Accident.”

Disagree.

The policy requires that the accident result in a **permanent and total loss of use** of a limb. Here, the doctor says Penelope **would regain full functionality** with three months of physical therapy, so the loss of use is not “permanent” as defined—even if she can’t afford the therapy and ends up with lasting impairment.

Disagree.

Even though the restaurant’s damage is “third party property” and Dave is legally liable to pay for it, the policy excludes coverage if he “failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage.” Since Dave knew for months that the brake warning light was on and the brakes were unresponsive but didn’t get the car serviced, the insurer can deny the claim under that exclusion.

**Agree.**

Dave is **legally liable** for damage to a **third party’s property** (the restaurant’s landscaping), which fits the policy definition. The exclusion only applies if he **failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage**—but he followed scheduled maintenance and checked his manual, which are reasonable preventive steps.

**Disagree.**

Although the restaurant’s landscaping is “third party property” and Dave is legally liable for the damage, the policy excludes coverage if Dave **failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the damage**. Since Dave knew the car was overdue for scheduled maintenance and still drove it, the insurer can argue he did not take reasonable steps to prevent brake failure and the resulting damage.

Disagree.

Although winds exceeding 55 mph were present, the roof damage was caused by Tom dropping the toolbox due to his phone ringing—not by the wind itself. Under the definition, coverage applies to “damage from wind,” and this damage was not from wind. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Agree. The damage was caused by wind with gusts in excess of 55 mph, which meets the policy’s definition of “Wind Damage.”

Disagree.

Even though the wind gusts exceeded 55 mph, the policy definition covers “damage from wind speeds,” and here the immediate cause of the roof damage was waves (water impact) rather than wind directly. Under that definition alone, it’s not clearly “wind damage.”

Disagree.

Even though a storm (lightning) caused the power outage, the roof damage was caused by the fire Cam accidentally started, not directly by the storm. Under the definition “damage caused by a storm,” this looks like fire damage rather than storm damage.
**Agree.** The damage was caused by a storm (the lightning storm), so it falls within the policy’s definition of “Storm Damage.”

**Agree.**

The roof damage was ultimately caused by a **storm** (lightning striking the power line), which set off the chain of events (power surge → heater fire → roof burns). Under a definition as broad as “damage caused by a storm,” this fits. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

Based on the policy definition, “Hail Damage” is covered only if the hail is intense enough that it **causes damage to hard surfaces or breaks glass**. Rick’s claimed loss is **damage to plants**, and the facts given don’t indicate any damage to hard surfaces or broken glass. Therefore, under this definition, the plant damage would not qualify for coverage.
Agree

Agree.

The hail chipped Rick’s skylight window, which is damage to a hard surface/glass. The definition covers hail “of such intensity that it causes damage to hard surfaces or breaks glass,” and a chipped skylight fits that trigger even if the roof isn’t leaking.

**Disagree.** The damage was caused by people dancing and shaking the house, not by **ground heave of the site** (soil movement) or **landslip**, so it doesn’t fit the policy’s definition of “Ground Heave” damage.

Disagree.

An earthquake causes damage from seismic shaking, not “ground heave” (upward movement of the ground) or “landslip” (downward movement/slide of land). Based on the definition given, the loss isn’t clearly “resulting from ground heave … or landslip,” so it wouldn’t fall under this specific Ground Heave coverage.

Disagree.

“Ground heave” (and “landslip”) refers to movement of the ground itself (e.g., soil expansion, subsidence, slope movement). Here, the damage was caused by vibration/impact from a vehicle collision shaking the house, not by ground heave or landslip of the site.

Disagree.

The policy covers damage resulting from **ground heave of the site** or **landslip**. Clint’s roof collapse is described as being due to **water damage**, not ground movement (heave) or landslip, so it doesn’t fall within the Ground Heave definition.
**Agree.** The damage was caused by the ground beneath the house shifting (ground heave of the site on which the building stands), which matches the policy definition. Final answer is: **Agree**.
**Disagree.** The damage was caused by a tree falling onto the roof, not by **ground heave of the site** or a **landslip**, so it doesn’t fall within the policy’s “Ground Heave” definition.

**Disagree.** Trace and Access covers costs to locate and fix **accidental damage to cables or pipes**. Here, the pipe wasn’t accidentally damaged; Justin dropped his ring into it, and the drywall/floor damage was to retrieve the ring, not to fix pipe damage.

**Agree.** The drywall and floorboard removal are **necessary and reasonable costs** incurred to **locate and fix accidental damage to a pipe in his home**, which fits the Trace and Access definition.

Disagree.

The Trace and Access coverage applies to costs incurred to locate and fix **accidental damage to cables or pipes**. Here, the pipe damage was caused by **Justin’s own work while repairing the sink**, so it is not the kind of accidental damage contemplated for Trace and Access (which typically addresses hidden/unknown damage requiring locating and opening up).

Disagree.

“Trace and Access” covers costs to locate and fix **accidental damage** to the pipes/cables/tanks. Here, the low water pressure was caused by **Jett’s faulty/poor installation**, not accidental damage to the piping, so the trace/access work (like removing floorboards to inspect) would not be covered under that definition.

Disagree.

“Trace and Access” covers the costs of locating and fixing **accidental damage** to the pipes (typically damage caused while accessing them), not the cost to repair the pipes **themselves** when they crack due to freezing. Here, the pipes cracked from freezing rather than being accidentally damaged during tracing/access.

**Disagree.**

The Trace and Access cover applies to costs of locating and fixing **accidental damage** to pipes/cables/drains/tanks. Here, the pipes **cracked over time due to long-term wear** (a gradual deterioration/maintenance issue), not accidental damage. Therefore, Jett would not be covered under this Trace and Access definition.

**Disagree.**

At the time of the incident, the car was under the **custody/control of Jenny’s friend with Jenny’s permission**, and the friend is **not covered under the policy**. The exclusion therefore applies, so the Vehicle Damage coverage would not pay for this loss.

Agree.

At the time of the incident, Jenny’s car was not under the custody or control of anyone else with her permission (it was just parked outside), so the exclusion doesn’t apply. The damage from the falling utility pole is therefore covered under the Vehicle Damage policy. Final answer is: **Agree**.

**Agree.**

The exclusion applies only if, at the time of the incident, the car was **under the custody or control** of someone with Jenny’s permission who is **not covered**. Here, the neighbor merely held the key for safekeeping while the car was parked and was not exercising custody/control over the car when a utility pole fell on it. The damage arose from an external event, not from the neighbor’s control of the vehicle.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Agree

The damage to John’s front left wheel was caused by a minor accident, not by wear and tear, so it falls within “Vehicle Damage” coverage and is not excluded.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**

Disagree.  

The policy covers damage to (1) Steve’s car or (2) car accessories while in or on the car. A guitar is personal property, not a car accessory, so it wouldn’t fall under “Vehicle Damage” as defined.  

Final answer is: **Disagree**

Agree

Because the GPS navigation system is a **car accessory** and it was **in the car** at the time of the accident, it falls within the policy’s definition of “Vehicle Damage” (“car accessories (while in or on the car)”).

Disagree.

The policy covers damage to (1) the car or (2) “car accessories (while in or on the car).” A tablet computer—even if used for navigation—is not typically considered a “car accessory” under this definition, so it would not be covered as Vehicle Damage. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Disagree.

Agree

Because the policy’s “Vehicle Damage” coverage includes damage to “audio equipment (while in the car),” and Chris’s stereo system and speakers (audio equipment) were damaged in the accident while in the car, the claim falls within the definition.

Disagree.

The policy covers damage to (1) the car or (2) audio equipment **while in the car**. A portable Bluetooth speaker isn’t clearly “audio equipment” in the sense of in-car audio equipment, and the definition doesn’t explicitly include portable personal devices.

Disagree.

The policy covers car accessories (including children’s car seats) only **“while in or on the car.”** Once James removed the car seat and placed it on the trunk, it was no longer **in or on the car**, so the theft is not covered under this “Vehicle Damage” definition. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers car accessories (including children’s car seats) only **“while in or on the car.”** At the time of the crash, the car seat was on the **trunk** (i.e., on the car), but the damage was caused by another car crashing into the parked car—so the seat was still “on the car” when damaged.  

Actually, that means the condition is satisfied. Therefore:

Final answer: **Agree**.

Disagree.  

The policy covers car accessories (including children’s car seats) only **“while in or on the car.”** At the time of the accident, the car seat was on the **trunk**, which is part of the car, so it was **on the car** and would fall within the definition of Vehicle Damage coverage.
Disagree. The policy covers theft of the car itself or the car’s audio equipment (while in or on the car). The stolen item was a briefcase (and laptop), not the vehicle or audio equipment.

Agree

The policy covers loss or damage resulting from theft to the policyholder’s car **or audio equipment (while in or on the car)**. The stereo was stolen from inside Gisele’s car, so it falls within the definition of “Vehicle Theft” coverage. Final answer is: **Agree**

**Disagree.**

The policy covers theft of **the car** or **audio equipment (while in or on the car)**. A **wireless Bluetooth headset** is not clearly “audio equipment” of the car (it’s typically personal portable electronics), so under the given definition, the theft wouldn’t be covered.

**Disagree.**

The “Vehicle Fire” coverage applies to loss or damage **to the car or its audio equipment** (while in or on the car) **as a result of fire**. Here, the loss Gene is claiming is for **bills/paper documents**, which are neither the car nor audio equipment. So that loss isn’t covered by this definition, even though a fire occurred in the car.

**Agree.**

The policy covers “loss or damage as a result of fire to the policy holder’s car or audio equipment (while in or on the car).” Here, a fire occurred and it damaged Gene’s car audio equipment (a stereo speaker) while it was in the car, so that damage falls within the definition of “Vehicle Fire” coverage.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers loss or damage from fire to **the car** or **audio equipment**. Here, the fire damage Gene is claiming is to a **smartphone** and a **portable Bluetooth speaker**. A smartphone is not clearly “audio equipment,” and the speaker was left **next to the phone** (not necessarily “in or on the car” as required by the definition). Therefore, based on the definition given, Gene is **not covered**.

Disagree.

The policy covers “loss or damage … as a result of **fire, lightning or explosion**.” Here, there was **no fire**—only smoke damage from a smoldering cigarette butt that did not ignite—so the damage doesn’t fall within the defined “Vehicle Fire” coverage. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
**Agree.** The loss to Ken’s car was “as a result of fire,” so it falls within the Vehicle Fire coverage definition, even though the fire started at the neighbor’s home. Final answer: **Agree**.

Disagree.  

The policy covers loss or damage to the car “as a result of fire.” Here, the car itself did not catch fire; only the smartphone did, and the damage to the car was from smoke. Under this definition, the loss is not “as a result of fire” to the vehicle (and it’s not lightning or explosion), so the Vehicle Fire coverage would not apply. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Disagree.

Agree.  

The seat covers are **car accessories** and were **in/on the car** when they were stolen, so the loss is “as a result of theft” to covered property under the Vehicle Theft definition.

Disagree.

The policy covers theft of the car or **car accessories (while in or on the car)**. A smartphone—even if used for navigation/music—is typically considered personal property, not a car accessory, so its theft wouldn’t fall under “Vehicle Theft” as defined. Final answer is: **Disagree**.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

**Agree.** The theft was committed by an unfamiliar person, not a family/household member or an employee/ex-employee, so the exclusion doesn’t apply and Dillon is covered under the Vehicle Theft policy. Final answer is: **Agree**.
Disagree. Final answer is: **Disagree**.

Disagree.

Because the “Vehicle Theft” coverage applies **only if the policyholder’s car contains a working GPS tracking device**, and Cooper’s car **did not** have one, the condition for coverage isn’t met—even though the car was clearly stolen.

Agree.

The car was stolen (someone broke in and drove it away), and Cooper’s car contained a working GPS tracking device, satisfying the policy’s stated condition for “Vehicle Theft” coverage. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

The policy covers vehicle theft only if the car contains a working GPS tracking device. A GPS-tracked smartphone is not necessarily a dedicated GPS tracking device installed in or intended to track the vehicle, so Cooper doesn’t clearly meet the stated condition for coverage.

Disagree.

The policy covers “broken glass” in the windows or windshield. Here, the windshield is not broken—there’s only a minor cosmetic scratch—so it doesn’t meet the policy’s defined covered damage.
Agree.

Disagree.

The policy defines covered “Vehicle Glass” damage as **“broken glass”** in the windows or windshield. A **chip** where the windshield remains **intact and functional** is not clearly “broken glass” under that definition, so the claim would likely fall outside the stated coverage.
Disagree.

**Agree.** The windshield is **broken glass**, and the scratches were caused **solely and directly by the broken glass shards**, so both types of damage fit the policy’s “Vehicle Glass” definition. Final answer is: **Agree**.

Disagree.

The “Vehicle Glass” coverage is defined as **broken glass** and **scratching to the bodywork caused solely and directly by broken glass**. Here, the primary damage is to the **side mirror** (generally not considered “vehicle glass” under this definition), and the scratches were caused by glass shards from the mirror rather than from covered “vehicle glass.”

**Agree.**

The policy covers damage caused when first responders force entry **because of an emergency or perceived emergency involving the policyholder**. Joanne **knew** the noises were just her dogs playing and that there was no emergency involving her, so the forced entry was not due to an emergency (or perceived emergency) of that kind under the definition. Final answer is: **Agree**.
Disagree.

**Disagree.**

The policy covers damage caused when first responders force entry **because of an emergency or perceived emergency involving the policyholder**. Joanne genuinely believed there was a burglar and called the police, so the police forced entry due to a **perceived emergency** involving her, even though it later turned out there was no actual burglar.

**Agree.**

The policy covers damage caused when **first responders** force entry due to an emergency involving Salma. Sam is an **accountant** (not a first responder), so the damage from his forced entry does not meet the definition of “Emergency Damages,” even though there was a real burglar.

**Disagree.** The forced entry was made by first responders due to an emergency involving Salma (a burglar in her home), so the resulting damage fits the policy’s definition of covered “Emergency Damages.”
